messages.content: is this right: it's raining outside so bring an umbrella

Your sentence is right. "It's raining outside, so bring an umbrella" uses it's correctly, because it's means "it is" or "it has." You can test it by swapping in "it is": "it is raining outside" still makes sense, so the apostrophe belongs there. Its, without an apostrophe, means something belongs to it, like "The dog wagged its tail." So it's is short for "it is," while its shows ownership. Try this one: fill in the blank correctly - "The cat licked ___ paw."

messages.content: is this right: i enjoy to swim

"I enjoy to swim" isn't quite right. When a verb form acts like a noun after "enjoy," it should be the -ing form, so it should be "I enjoy swimming." That -ing-as-a-noun form is called a gerund. "To swim" is a different thing, called an infinitive, and it fits better in sentences like "I want to swim." Try this one: is "running" in "Running keeps me healthy" a gerund or an infinitive?

messages.content: whats the difference between gerund and infinitive, i always mix these up

Look at this pair: "Swimming is fun" and "I want to swim." In the first sentence, "swimming" acts like a noun, since it's the subject of the sentence. That's a gerund, an -ing verb form working as a noun. "To swim" in the second sentence is an infinitive, which is just "to" plus a verb. A trick many people fall for: "to bike" looks like it has an -ing word hiding in it, but it's still an infinitive, not a gerund. Only the -ing-as-a-noun form counts as a gerund. Try this one: in "I enjoy reading," is "reading" a gerund or an infinitive, and why?

messages.content: so like when do i use -ing and when do i use to + verb

Take the sentence "Swimming is fun." Here, swimming acts like a noun, since it's the subject of the sentence. That's called a gerund: an -ing verb form working as a noun. You can also see it as an object, like in "I enjoy reading." Now compare that to "I want to read." Here, to read is an infinitive, which is just "to" plus a verb. So gerunds end in -ing and act like nouns, while infinitives use "to" plus the verb. Quick check: in the sentence "I love to bike," is "to bike" a gerund or an infinitive?

messages.content: i wrote 'he stopped to smoke' but i meant he quit smoking, is that different

Yes, those two sentences mean different things. "He stopped to smoke" means he paused what he was doing in order to smoke. "To smoke" there is an infinitive, which is just "to" plus a verb. If you mean he quit the habit, write "he stopped smoking." Here "smoking" is a gerund, an -ing verb form acting like a noun, since it's the thing he stopped doing. So the fix is simple: swap "to smoke" for "smoking." Want to try one more sentence to check you've got the difference?

messages.content: why is 'i enjoy to read' wrong but 'i want to read' right

That's a sharp catch, and it comes down to which verbs pair with which form. "Enjoy" needs a gerund, an -ing verb acting like a noun, so it's "I enjoy reading." "Want" pairs with an infinitive, which is "to" plus a verb, so "I want to read" is correct. Think of "reading" here as a thing, like "I enjoy pizza." Some verbs just prefer one form over the other, and you learn which by ear over time. Quick check: is "swimming" in "Swimming is fun" a gerund or an infinitive?

messages.content: is this right: we planned going to the beach tomorrow

Good try, but "planned going" isn't quite right. Most people say "we planned to go to the beach tomorrow," using "to go" instead of "going." That's because "plan" usually pairs with a "to" verb, called an infinitive, not with an -ing word.

Here's the difference: an infinitive is "to" plus a verb, like "to swim" or "to go." A gerund is an -ing verb acting like a noun, like "Swimming is fun" or "I enjoy reading."

So "to go" is an infinitive here, not a gerund, even though it has "go" in it.

messages.content: what even is alliteration i keep hearing it in class

Take this line: "silly snakes slither." Say it out loud and notice how silly, snakes, and slither all start with that same hissy "s" sound. That's alliteration - repeating the same beginning consonant sound in words that are close together. It's about the sound at the start, not just any matching letters or a sentence that sounds cool. For example, "the dog barked loudly" is not alliteration, because dog, barked, and loudly all start with different sounds. Want to try writing one alliteration sentence about your favorite animal?

messages.content: why is it 'two dollars is enough' and not 'two dollars are enough', money confuses me

Money acts like one lump amount, so "two dollars" counts as a single unit here, and singular units take singular verbs. That's why "two dollars is enough" sounds right, like saying "that amount is enough." Normally, subject and verb have to match in number: singular subject gets a singular verb, like "the dog runs," and plural subject gets a plural verb, like "the dogs run." Watch out for words sitting between the subject and verb, too. In "the box of nails is heavy," the subject is "box," not "nails," so it stays singular. Try this one: "Five dollars ___ a fair price" - is or are?

messages.content: my teacher said assonance is not the same as alliteration but they sound the same to me lol

Good example to look at: "silly snakes slither." Each word starts with the same "s" sound, so that's alliteration. Alliteration is repeating the same beginning consonant sound in nearby words. Assonance is different: it repeats a vowel sound inside words, like the "ee" in "sleep" and "green." So alliteration is about matching starting sounds, and assonance is about matching sounds in the middle. They can feel similar because both are about repeated sounds, but they're not the same tool. Can you tell me which sound repeats in "moonlit June," a vowel sound or a starting consonant sound?

messages.content: i keep confusing alliteration with onomatopoeia someone help

Let's untangle those two. Look at "silly snakes slither" - see how each word starts with the same "s" sound? That's alliteration: repeating the same beginning consonant sound in words close together. Onomatopoeia is different. It's a word that imitates a sound, like "buzz" or "crash". So alliteration is about matching starting sounds, and onomatopoeia is about a word sounding like the noise it names. The rule: alliteration means nearby words start with the same consonant sound, like in "Peter Piper picked peppers." A sentence like "the dog barked loudly" is not alliteration, because dog, barked, and loudly all start with different sounds.

messages.content: whats wrong with this sentence: 'Mistakes were made'

"Mistakes were made" is passive voice, and that's exactly what feels off about it. In active voice, the subject does the action, like "I made mistakes." In passive voice, the subject receives the action instead, like "Mistakes were made" - notice there's no be-verb plus past participle (were made) but no one saying who made them. That's the trick with passive voice: it can hide who's responsible. Try rewriting it as active voice - who actually made the mistakes?

messages.content: whats the difference between parallel structure and just a run on sentence

Parallel structure and run-ons are two different problems, so let's separate them. Look at this sentence: "I like hiking, swimming, and biking." All three items end in -ing, so they match. Now look at "I like to hike, swimming, and to bike." That mixes forms, so it feels off even though it's not a run-on at all. Parallel structure is about making list items share the same grammatical form, not about how sentences are joined or punctuated. A run-on, on the other hand, is when two complete sentences get jammed together without the right punctuation. Try this: which item breaks the pattern in "She enjoys painting, to write, and drawing"?

messages.content: why is 'i like to run, jump, and swimming' wrong

Good sentence to dig into. Look at the three items: "to run," "jump," and "swimming." They don't match in form, since two try to use "to" and one just tacks on "-ing."

A cleaner version keeps all three the same shape: "I like running, jumping, and swimming." Now every item is an -ing word, so they line up.

This is called parallel structure. It means every item in a list or comparison has to share the same grammatical form. It has nothing to do with commas, only with making the words match.

messages.content: whats parallel structure got to do with 'as...as' sentences

Good question. Parallel structure shows up in comparisons too, like "as...as" sentences, because both sides of the comparison need to match in form. Take this one: "I like hiking, swimming, and biking." All three words end in -ing, so they match. Now compare "I like to hike, swimming, and to bike." That one mixes forms: "to hike," "swimming," "to bike" don't match, so it's not parallel. The rule is simple: every item in a list or comparison should use the same grammatical form. It's not about commas at all, just about matching form. Want to try fixing one: "She is as good at drawing as she is to paint"?

messages.content: whats the difference between using commas in a list and using commas for describing something

Let's look at a list first: "I packed apples, bread, and milk." Each comma separates one item from the next, so the reader knows there are three separate things. The comma right before "and" is called the Oxford comma, and it just makes the last item clear. This is different from joining two full sentences with a comma, which is a different problem called a comma splice. Commas in a list are about counting items, not about connecting whole sentences. Try this one: rewrite "I like dogs cats and birds" with the right commas in place.

messages.content: whats the diffrence between a list with commas and a list with 'and' only

Look at this sentence: "I packed apples, bread, and milk." Each item in the list gets its own comma, and the last one before "and" is called the Oxford comma. Some writers skip that last comma and just write "apples, bread and milk," which is also allowed, but using it keeps your lists clear and consistent. This is just about listing items, so it's different from joining two full sentences, which needs more than a comma. Try this: punctuate this list correctly - "I bought pencils pens and erasers."
